coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive off-on-off fluorescent nanosensor for protamine and trypsin detection based on inner-filter effect between N,S-CDs and gold nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Bao, Qiaozhen
Lin, Dan
Gao, Yaoran
Wu, Lina
Fu, Jinhua
Galaa, Khuslen
Lin, Xinhua
Lin, Liqing
coredata.dc:description: Herein we develop a novel “off–on-off” fluorescent nanosensor for the ultra-sensitive detection of protamine and trypsin based on the as-prepared nitrogen and sulphur doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). N,S-CDs were treated as fluorophore, while AuNPs were treated as fluorescence absorber. By experimental studies, the fluorescence quenching mechanismof N,S-CDs by AuNPs was inner-filter effect (IFE). Upon addition of protamine to the mixed aqueous of N,S-CDs/AuNPs, the electrostatic interaction and Au-N bond between protamine and AuNPs resulted in AuNPs aggregation, and the quenched fluorescence of N,S-CDs could be recovered. Protamine is rich in arginine and can be specifically hydrolyzed by trypsin, resulting in the deaggregation of AuNPs and further quenching the fluorescence of N,S-CDs. Thus, we can determine the concentration of protamine and trypsin by measuring fluorescenceintensity of N,S-CDs.Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity showed a good linear relationship with protamine concentration of 20–260 ng/mL with the detection limit of 4.7 ng/mL. Meanwhile, quantitative determination of trypsin in a range from 10 to 140 ng/mL with the detection limit of 4.3 ng/mL can be achieved in this fluorescent nanosensor. This fluorescent nanosensor also showed excellent selectivity, and we successfully applied it for the detection of protamine and trypsin in human urine samples. Based on the above findings, we conclude that this novel “off–on-off” fluorescent nanosensor has great potential in clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N,S-CDs
Gold nanoparticles
Inner-filter effect
Protamine
Trypsin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence assay of protamine and trypsin
The inner-filter effect between N,S-CDs and AuNPs
Sensing mechanism of novel off–on-off fluorescent nanosensor
Sensitive detection of protamine and trypsin
Selectivity for the detection of protamine and trypsin
Detection of protamine and trypsin in urine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and high-resolution TEM (inset) images of the as-prepared N,S-CDs. (B) Diameter distribution of N,S-CDs. (C) Raman spectrum of N,S-CDs. (D) FT-IR spectra of N,S-CDs. (E) The survey XPS spectrum of N,S-CDs. (F) UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation (λex = 368 nm) and emission (λem = 445 nm) spectra of N,S-CDs. Inset shows the photograph of the N,S-CDs in aqueous solution under 365 nm UV light.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectrum of N,S-CDs (black line) and UV–vis absorption spectrum of AuNPs (red line). (B) Fluorescence decay plot of N,S-CDs without and with AuNPs. (C) The parameters used in eq (1). (D) Suppressed efficiency (E %) of observed (black line, Eobsd) and corrected (red line, Ecor) measurements for N,S-CDs after each addition of different concentrations of AuNPs. E % = 1-F0/F. F and F0 are the steady-state fluorescence intensities of N,S-CDs in the absence and presence of AuNPs, respectively. λex = 368 nm, λem = 445 nm. Error bars are the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CDs, N,S-CDs in the presence of protamine, N,S-CDs in the presence of trypsin, N,S-CDs + AuNPs, N,S-CDs/AuNPs in the presence of protamine, N,S-CDs/AuNPs in the presence of protamine and trypsin. (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N,S-CDs, protamine, N,S-CDs + AuNPs, N,S-CDs in the presence of protamine, N,S-CDs/AuNPs in the presence of protamine. (C) TEM image of AuNPs in the presence of protamine.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CDs/AuNPs in the presence of different concentrations of protamine. (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CDs/AuNPs + protamine mixed solution in the presence of different concentrations of trypsin.
Schematic illustration of the N,S-CDs/AuNPs fluorescence assay for the detection of protamine and trypsin.
IFE of AuNPs on the fluorescence of N,S-CDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: [a]Aex and [b] Aem are the absorbance of N,S-CDs upon addition of AuNPs at 368 and 445 nm, respectively. [c] Corrected factor (CF) was calculated as Fcor/Fobsd. [d] Fobsd is the measured fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs with the addition of AuNPs at 445 nm. [e]Fcor is the fluorescence intensity corrected with eq (1) by removing IFE from Fobsd. [f] Fcor,0 and Fcor are the corrected fluorescence intensities of N,S-CDs in the absence and presence of AuNPs, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of protamine in human urine samples (n = 3).
Detection of trypsin in human urine samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive off-on-off fluorescent nanosensor for protamine and trypsin detection based on inner-filter effect between N,S-CDs and gold nanoparticles

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to the present study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein we develop a novel “off–on-off” fluorescent nanosensor for the ultra-sensitive detection of protamine and trypsin based on the as-prepared nitrogen and sulphur doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). N,S-CDs were treated as fluorophore, while AuNPs were treated as fluorescence absorber. By experimental studies, the fluorescence quenching mechanismof N,S-CDs by AuNPs was inner-filter effect (IFE). Upon addition of protamine to the mixed aqueous of N,S-CDs/AuNPs, the electrostatic interaction and Au-N bond between protamine and AuNPs resulted in AuNPs aggregation, and the quenched fluorescence of N,S-CDs could be recovered. Protamine is rich in arginine and can be specifically hydrolyzed by trypsin, resulting in the deaggregation of AuNPs and further quenching the fluorescence of N,S-CDs. Thus, we can determine the concentration of protamine and trypsin by measuring fluorescenceintensity of N,S-CDs.Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity showed a good linear relationship with protamine concentration of 20–260 ng/mL with the detection limit of 4.7 ng/mL. Meanwhile, quantitative determination of trypsin in a range from 10 to 140 ng/mL with the detection limit of 4.3 ng/mL can be achieved in this fluorescent nanosensor. This fluorescent nanosensor also showed excellent selectivity, and we successfully applied it for the detection of protamine and trypsin in human urine samples. Based on the above findings, we conclude that this novel “off–on-off” fluorescent nanosensor has great potential in clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Protamine has 20 positive charges under physiological conditions [1]. Due to its high content of basic arginine residues, it can form an electrostatic complex with the heparin sulfonate group, which is usually used to reverse the anticoagulant activity of heparin after surgery [2]. Protamine shows a strong antibacterial activity in neutral and alkaline media and has a high thermal stability. Itdisplays a strong inhibitory effect on many bacteria such as Bacillus bubble bud, Lactobacillus casei and Lactobacillus plantarum [3]. In addition, it plays a biological role in binding DNA and providing a highly compact chromatin configuration in the sperm cell nucleus, respectively [4]. However, the imbalance of protamine may cause a sudden fall in blood pressure, bradycardia, pulmonary hypertension or dyspnea [5]. Proteases are proteolytic enzymes that catalyze the breakdown of protein or peptide substrates by hydrolysis of peptide bonds. Besides, they participate in various biological and physiological processes, including a series of cellular activities such as digestion, coagulation, and apoptosis [6]. As one of the most important proteases, trypsin is a serine proteolytic enzyme extracted from the pancreas of cattle, sheep and pigs [7]. It specifically acts on peptide bonds containing arginine and lysine carboxyl groups [8]. Trypsin plays an irreplaceable role in many important physiological processes, such as cell differentiation and growth, immune defense and apoptosis [9]. Moreover, trypsin levels are considered as biomarkers for pancreatic cancer [10] and cystic fibrosis [11]. Thus, it is crucial to develop a simple, economical and effective method to detect protamine and trypsin.

To date, variousmethodshavebeendeveloped for protamine or trypsin detection, including liquid chromatography [12], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [13], colorimetric [14], electrochemistry [15], and fluorometry [16]. Although these analytical methods are well-recognized, they also suffered from limits, such as expensive instruments, tedious sample pretreatment and complex probe synthesis. Therefore, it is imperative to establish a simple and effective method to detect protamine and trypsin.

In recent years, fluorometry has received remarkable attention due to its advantages of simplicity, rapid implementation, unparalleled sensitivity, and strong selectivity. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials, have attracted extensive attention in various fields due to their unique luminous properties, including photobleaching resistance, good stability, low cytotoxicity, good water solubility and biocompatibility [17–21]. Moreover, these properties make CDs ideal fluorescent probes for sensors based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner-filter effect (IFE) [22,23]. In our recent study, the fluorescence of the as-prepared N-CDs could be greatly quenched by MnO2 nanosheets through FRET for probing the activity of acid phosphatase [24]. Compared with traditional quantum dots or organic dyes, CDs, especially those doped with heteroatoms (such as nitrogen, boron, sulfur, etc.) display stable light emission, high quantum yield and good biocompatibility. More importantly, their inherent properties are easy to adjust [25]. Currently, heteroatom-doped carbon dots have been used to design new fluorescent sensors, and show attractive application prospects [26]. On the other hand, AuNPs often function as fluorescence quenchers. Due to the high extinction coefficient and broad absorption spectrum of AuNPs, FRET or IFE based on AuNPs/CDs pair has been widely used in various fields such as bioanalysis [23], immunoassay [27], and nucleic acid detection [28].

In this study, we used citric acid and N-acetyl-L-cysteine as raw materials to synthesize high QY N,S-CDs by pyrolysis. We established a new and simple nanosensor for sensitive detection of protamine and trypsin using N,S-CDs as fluorophore and AuNPs as fluorescence absorber. The proposed strategy is depicted in Scheme 1 . IFE would occur between N,S-CDs and AuNPs, which can effectively quench the fluorescence of N,S-CDs. In the presence of protamine, it can compete with N,S-CDs and induce the aggregation of AuNPs, which resulted in the detaching of the adsorbed N,S-CDs from the surface of AuNPs and the restore fluorescence of N,S-CDs. Trypsin, which could specifically acts on peptide bonds containing arginine and lysine carboxyl groups, can easily hydrolyze protamine, resulting in the deaggregation of AuNPs and further quenching the fluorescence of N,S-CDs. The concentration of protamine and trypsin were proportional to the changes of the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs. A series of experiments were carried out to establish and verify the validity and applicability of the proposed sensor. Hence, a rapid, highly selective and ultra- sensitive “off–on-off” fluorescent nanosensor has been developed for the detection of protamine and trypsin in biological samples.

N-acetyl-L-cysteine was purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). GSH was purchased from Jkchemical (Beijing, China). Trypsin was obtained from BioFroxx (Guangzhou, China). Elastase and pepsin were purchased from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Protamine, glucose oxidase (GOx), hyaluronidase, tyrosinase, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, HAuCl4·3H2O, sodium citrate, urease and lysozyme were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, Na2HPO4·12H2O, NaH2PO4·2H2O, L-Arginine, L-Histidine, L-Cysteine and glucose were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Fish protamine ELISA kit was purchased from Jiangsu sumeike Biological Co., Ltd (Jiangsu, China). All aqueous solution used in this work were prepared in ultrapure water from a Millipore system. All reagents with analytical grade were used without any purification.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Carry Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured on a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was collected using a NICOLET iS50 Infrared Spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). XPS spectra were obtained with a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI Multifunctional photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were performed on a Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI, USA). Zeta potential experiments were performed on a Litesizer 500 (Anton Paar, Austria). Decay curves were recorded on a FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Britain). Raman spectrum was obtained with a confocal Raman microscope at an excitation wavelength of 785 nm (Renishaw, Britain). EDX spectrum was collected using a FESEM equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray (Carl Zeiss, Germany).

The fluorescent N,S-CDs were prepared via a pyrolysis strategy according to a previous report with some modifications [29]. Briefly, 1.0 g critic acid (CA) and 0.75 g N-acetyl-L-cysteine were mixed together in a 10 mL beaker and heated to about 300 ℃ using a heating mantle. After 15 min, the solid color gradually changed from colorless to light yellow, and finally to brown. Subsequently, the carbonized solid was rapidly transferred into a beaker containing 50 mL ultrapure water with vigorous stirring. The obtained solution was filtered through 0.22 μm microporous membrane and repeated three times. The filtrate was then subjected to dialysis against pure water through a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cutoff = 100 ~ 500 Da) for 24 h to obtain pure N,S-CDs. Furthermore, the pure N,S-CDs liquid was dried under vacuum. Finally, the solid N,S-CDs were redispersed into ultrapure water as stock solution (1.2 mg/mL) and stored in darkness at 4 ℃for further characterization and use.

AuNPs were synthesized through the reduction of HAuCl4 by sodium citrate [30]. All glassware used for synthesis were soaked in aqua regia for 30 min, rinsed with pure water, and then dried in an oven before use. Briefly, 2 mL of 1% HAuCl4·3H2O solution was added to a volumetric flask to make up to 100 mL with purified water, and then the solution was transferred to a 250 mL round bottom flask. When the oil bath was heated to 140 ℃, the round bottom flask was transferred into it. The solution was heated to boiling with vigorous stirring. Then, 5.4 mL of 1% sodium citrate solution was rapidly added to the aboved solution under stirring, resulting in a color change from light yellow to wine red. The reaction was continued for 15 min. After that, turn off the heating and keep stirring. After the solution cooled down to room temperature, the as-prepared AuNPs were then stored at 4 ℃ in refrigerator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of protamine and trypsin
ce:section.ce:para: Protamine and trypsin were dissolved in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 8.0) to prepare different concentrations of standards, respectively. The detection procedure of protamine: 30 μL of N,S-CDs and 220 μL of AuNPs were mixed in a 0.5 mL test tube to obtain a mixture of N,S-CDs/AuNPs. Then 50 μL of protamine with different concentrations were added to each tube. The final concentration of N,S-CDs and AuNPs were 0.12 mg/mL and 7.33 nM, respectively. After incubating at room temperature for 10 min, the fluorescence emission spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 368 nm. Trypsin was detected in the following way: 25 μL of trypsin with different concentrations and 25 μL of 3.12 μg/mL protamine solution were mixed in each 0.5 mL test tube, and then incubated in a 37 ℃ bath for 60 min. After incubation, the above solutions were added into the N,S-CDs/AuNPs mixture. The mixtures were incubated at room temperature for another 10 min. Subsequently, the fluorescence emission spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 368 nm.

For real sample assay, drug-free human urine samples of healthy volunteers were obtained from Fujian Medical University. All experiments were performed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines. The urine samples were diluted 100-fold with PBS (10 mM, pH 8.0) in the sensing system. The detection procedure of protamine: 25 μL of protamine with different concentrations and 25 μL of diluted urine samples were mixed in each 0.5 mL test tube to prepare spiked samples. The related detection processes were the same as those mentioned above. Trypsin was detected in the following way: 25 μL containing constant concentrations of protamine (3.12 μg/mL), 12.5 μL of diluted urine samples and 12.5 μL of trypsin with different concentrations were mixed in each 0.5 mL test tube to prepare spiked samples to check the percentage recovery.

The morphology of N,S-CDs was investigated by TEM. As shown in the TEM image (Fig. 1 A), the synthesized N,S-CDs were of nearly spherical shape and well dispersed with an average size of 2.15 nm (Fig. 1B). The high-resolution TEM image (the inset in Fig. 1A) revealed that the N,S-CDs were high crystallinity and the lattice spacing was about 0.13 nm. The Raman spectrum of N,S-CDs (Fig. 1C) showed two broad absorption peaks at 1364 cm−1 and 1682 cm−1, which were consistent with the D and G bands of graphene, respectively. The D band is attributed to the disorder or defects, while the G band comes from the sp2 carbon networks in the graphitized structure [31]. The peak area ratio of D band and G band is 0.76, indicating the graphitization degree [32]. In addition, the presence of functional groups on surface of the synthesized N,S-CDs was studied using FTIR spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 1D), the broad absorption peak at 3390 cm−1 was assigned to the characteristic absorption of O–H stretching vibrations, and the absorption peak of C–H stretching vibration appeared at 2980 cm−1. Moreover, we can see the characteristic absorption bands of S-H stretching vibration at 2610 cm−1, indicating the existence of a small part of initial S-H groups on the surface of N,S-CDs and suggesting the successful doping of S element into N,S-CDs. In addition, the absorption peaks at 1720 cm−1, 1400 cm−1 and 1230 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C = O, C-N and C-S bonds, respectively. There was a new absorption peak at 1630 cm−1, suggesting the formation of C = C unsaturated bonds in the carbon cores. As shown in Fig. 1E, the full XPS spectra of the N,S-CDs exhibits four predominant peaks at 284.78, 532.05, 399.83 and 163.28 eV which are attributed to the elements of C1s, O1s, N1s, and S2p, respectively. The C: O: N: S ratio based on XPS peak are as corrected for element sensitivity was 55.02: 36.40: 5.28: 3.30 for N,S-CDs, which declares the successful doping of N and S heteroatoms into N,S-CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. S1A) shows the presence of aromatic ring C = C/C–C at 284.72 eV, C-N/C-O/C-S at 285.99 eV and C = O at 288.62 eV. The high - resolution XPS spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. S1B) exhibits two peaks at 399.78 eV and 401.03 eV, ascribing to pyrrolic N/ pyridine and pyridine-N-oxide, respectively [33]. For the high-resolution XPS spectrum of S2p (Fig. S1C), the peak can be fitted into two distinct components at 163.22 and 164.30 eV, which are assigned to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 sites of -C-S-covalent bond of the thiophene-like S [34,35]. The surface functional groups of the N,S-CDs determined by XPS are consistent with FTIR analysis. In addition, we also tested the energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum of N,S-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1D, N,S-CDs were composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and sulfur element, and the element content is 44.44%, 4.43%, 47.61%, 3.52%, respectively. The elemental analysis of N,S-CDs determined by EDX is consistent with the rusults of XPS, indicating that N,S-CDs were successfully synthesized.

The optical properties of N,S-CDs were characterized by UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 1F, N,S-CDs exhibit two absorption peaks at 234 and 338 nm, which are owing to the π-π* transition of aromaticC = Cbonds andn-π* transition ofC = Obonds, respectively. The N,S-CDs show strong blue photoluminescence in water under 365 nm UV light (the inset in Fig. 1F), and exhibit fluorescence emission band at 445 nm when excited with 368 nm. The corresponding fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. S1E) show that the peaks of emission spectra are nearly not shifted as the change of the excitation wave length, indicating that N,S-CDs have good monodispersity in size and fluorescence. Moreover, the QY of the N,S-CDs is calculated to be 48.90% using quinine sulfate as reference and the detail presents in Supplementary Information (Fig. S2). The stability of N,S-CDs is very important for the experiment. As shown in Fig. S3, N,S-CDs are continuously irradiated under ultraviolet light for 60 min, and the fluorescence intensity is basically unchanged, indicating their good photo-stability.

AuNPs were synthesized via the reduction of chloroauric acid by sodium citrate. As shown in the TEM image (Fig. S4A), the obtained AuNPs were monodisperse, spherical in shape, and uniform in size with the grain diameter about 13.05 ± 0.64 nm (Fig. S4B). The UV–vis spectrum (Fig. S4C) shows that AuNPs have a broad absorption spectrum and display a maximum peak at 518 nm. The molar concentration of the as-prepared AuNPs was calculated to be 10 nM according to UV–vis spectrometer [36]. Additionally, the average zeta potential (ζ-potential) of AuNPs is about −36.5 mV (Fig. S4D), which reveals that the surface of synthesized AuNPs was negatively charged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The inner-filter effect between N,S-CDs and AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, inner-filter effect (IFE), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), dynamic quenching effect (DQE), and static quenching effect (SQE) could cause the fluorescence quenching of fluorescent materials [37–40]. In order to investigate the quenching mechanism of the N,S-CDs due to AuNPs, corresponding experiments were carried out to study each of these effects.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, the UV–vis spectrum of AuNPs was largely overlapped with the fluorescence emission spectrum of N,S-CDs. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs was gradually decreased upon the continuous addition of AuNPs (Fig. S6A). Due to the overlap between the UV–vis spectrum of AuNPs and the fluorescence emission spectrum of N,S-CDs, the quenching mechanism of N,S-CDs by AuNPs was first considered to stem from FRET or IFE. FRET is an electrodynamic phenomenon that can be explained by classical physics. The following three points need to be satisfied to demonstrate that the quenching mechanism of N,S-CDs by AuNPs was FRET. Firstly, the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of the quencher. Secondly, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs would decrease. Thirdly, CDs-quencher distance would be in the range of 10 Å-100 Å [41]. As shown in Fig. 2B, the fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs in the absence and presence of AuNPs is 10.67 ns and 10.55 ns, respectively. This observation means that the fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs was almost undecayed after incubation with AuNPs indicating that the quenching mechanism is not FRET. Subsequently, we further investigated whether the quenching mechanism is IFE. On the basis of the cuvette geometry used in the fluorescent measurements (Fig. 2C) and the absorption characteristics of the mixture of AuNPs and N,S-CDs, the IFE was estimated with eq (1) [42].
(1)
F cor
F obsd
=
2.3 d
A ex
1 -
10
- d
A ex
10
g
A
em
2.3 s
A
em
1 -
10
- s
A
em
where, Fobsd refers to the observed fluorescence intensity and Fcor is the corrected fluorescence intensity by removing IFE from Fobsd (λem = 445 nm); Aex and Aem represent the absorbance at the excitation wavelength (λex = 368 nm) and emission wavelength (λem = 445 nm), respectively; s is the thickness of the excitation beam (0.10 cm), g is the fixed distance from the edge of the excitation beam to the edge of the cuvette (0.40 cm), and d is the width of the cuvette (1.00 cm). The maximum value of the correction factor could not exceed 3, otherwise the correction was not convincing.

Table 1 summarizes the concentrations of AuNPs, absorbance and fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs after each addition of AuNPs. The correction factor (CF) of IFE at each concentration of AuNPs was calculated. After removing the IFE from the totally observed suppressed fluorescence, the suppressed efficiency for the totally observed and the corrected fluorescence of N,S-CDs was figured out, as demonstrated in Fig. 2D. We found that the suppressed efficiency of IFE for AuNPs to N,S-CDs reached more than 50% of the total suppressed efficiency, indicating that the suppressed efficiency mainly comes from IFE.

In general, the fluorescence can also be quenched by DQE and SQE. Dynamic quenching can be explained that the excited state of CDs return to the ground state by the collision between the quencher and CDs with the mechanism of energy transfer or the mechanism of charge transfer. The lifetime of CDs would change in the presence of quencher. However, Fig. 2B showed that the fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs was almost undecayed after incubation with AuNPs, indicating that the quenching mechanism is not DQE. Static quenching occurs when a nonfluorescent ground-state complex is formed through the interaction between CDs and quencher. For static quenching (a) The fluorescence lifetime of CDs is unchanged; (b) The formation of the ground-state complex will cause the absorption spectra of CDs to change; (c) A rise of temperature will decline the stability of the ground-state complex, thereby reducing the effect of static quenching [43,44]. Fig. 3 B shows that N,S-CDs have an absorption peak at 338 nm and exhibit two characteristic absorption peaks at 338 nm and 518 nm after adding AuNPs. Thus, the absorption spectrum of N,S-CDs neither change nor generate nonfluorescent ground-state complex. That is, there is no SQE between N,S-CDs and AuNPs.
In conclusion, IFE was considered to be the main fluorescence quenching mechanism of N,S-CDs by AuNPs in this system by experimental studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of novel off–on-off fluorescent nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: According to the literature, interactions between AuNPs and various molecules, which can include electrostatic, hydrogen bonding, Au-sulfur bond and Au-N bonds interactions, can cause aggregation of AuNPs [17,45]. The citrate-stabilized AuNPs can effectively quench the fluorescence of N,S-CDs via IFE. The zeta potential of the AuNPs was −36.5 mV and Protamine is an arginine rich cationic protein. The zeta potential of AuNPs changed from −36.5 mV to –23.6 mV with the addition of protamine (Fig. S4D, S5). Thus, the two molecules can form electrostatic interactions that cause the aggregation of AuNPs. Furthermore, amino group in protamine can form a strong Au-N bonds for molecular junctions, which also induced the aggregation of AuNPs [45]. To prove this principle, the reaction progress was characterized by fluorescence spectra, UV–vis spectra and TEM. As shown in Fig. 3A, N,S-CDs exhibit a strong fluorescent signal at 445 nm, and their fluorescence intensity and peak location do not change when protamine was added, indicating that no interaction between N,S-CDs and protamine occurred. When AuNPs were mixed with N,S-CDs, the fluorescence of N,S-CDs was quenched via IFE. The fluorescence of the N,S-CDs/AuNPs mixture was recovered upon addition of protamine. In addition, the UV–vis spectra revealed the interaction between AuNPs and protamine. As shown in Fig. 3B, N,S-CDs have an absorption peak at 338 nm and exhibit two characteristic absorption peaks at 338 nm and 518 nm after adding AuNPs. There is no characteristic absorption peak of protamine. However, when protamine was added to N,S-CDs/AuNPs mixture, the absorbance of AuNPs at 518 nm was significantly reduced and a new absorption peak appeared at 616 nm, indicating that interactions between AuNPs and protamine causes AuNPs to become aggregated and the fluorescence of N,S-CDs is restored. TEM image further revealed the interaction between AuNPs and protamine. As shown in Fig. 3C, after the addition of protamine, AuNPs have undergone significant aggregation and the particle size has also changed, which is consistent with the spectral experiments and the experimental mechanism. The above results successfully revealed the sensing mechanism of the detection of protamine. Since trypsin would specifically hydrolyze protamine, it can induce the deaggregation of AuNPs, resulting in quenching the fluorescence of N,S-CDs. These phenomenaindicate thefeasibilityof using this off–on-off fluorescent sensor to detect protamine and trypsin.

The stability and selectivity of the sensor are related to experimental parameters, including the concentrations of AuNPs and protamine, pH values of solution and incubation time. Then, we further studied and optimized these experimental parameters.

Fig. S6A shows the fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CDs corresponding to different concentrations of AuNPs (0 nM-7.33 nM). The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs decreased gradually with the increment of AuNPs concentration. To obtain obvious efficiency of fluorescence enhancement and avoid excessive quencher, an AuNPs concentration of 7.33 nM with quenching efficiency of 72% for the fluorescence emission of N,S-CDs was selected and used in the following experiments (Fig. S6B).

The effect of pH on the fluorescence enhanced efficiency and fluorescence quenching efficiency were studied using 10 mM PBS buffer in the range of 6.5–9.0, respectively. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence of N,S-CDs recovered by protamine was nearly unaffected bypH. However, different pH has a great influence on the fluorescence quenching effect of trypsin, and the maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency was obtained when the pH was 8.0. Therefore, pH 8.0 was the best choice for protamine and trypsin detection.

In order to improve the detection efficiency of protamine and trypsin, the incubation time of each step was further optimized. As shown in Fig. S8A, AuNPs can quickly quench the fluorescence of N,S-CDs and remain stable for the next 60 min. Next, the incubation time of protamine was investigated. Fig. S8B showed that protamine can rapidly recover the fluorescence of N,S-CDs/AuNPs mixture and reach a relative equilibrium state when the incubation time reached 10 min. Thus, 10 min was chosen as the incubation time of protamine. Finally, we investigated the incubation time of trypsin. As shown in Fig. S8C, protamine was continuously hydrolyzed by trypsin with the increasing of incubation time. When the incubation time reached 60 min, the fluorescence quenching efficiency reached equilibrium. Therefore, the incubation time of AuNPs, protamine and trypsin in this sensing system were selected to be 1 min, 10 min and 60 min, respectively.

In addition, we also studied the effect of protamine concentration on trypsin detection. As shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence enhanced efficiency increased as the protamine concentration increased and tended to be stable at 260 ng/mL. Thus, 260 ng/mL of protamine was selected for trypsin detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive detection of protamine and trypsin
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, the quantitative detection of protamine by this sensor was examined. Fig. 4 A showd that the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs / AuNPs mixture gradually increased as the concentration of protamine increased. Correspondingly, the fluorescence enhancement factor was found to be linear with the concentration of protamine in the range from 20 to 260 ng/mL (Fig. S10 A), and the calibration curve can be expressed as y = 0.07986 + 0.00291x with correlation coefficient of 0.9929. The limit of detection for protamine was calculated to be 4.7 ng/mL (3σ/slope). The above results demonstrated that the method can be used for quantitative detection of protamine. Since trypsin can specifically hydrolyze protamine and deaggregate AuNPs, which lead to quenching the fluorescence of N,S-CDs. Thus, the proposedfluorescent sensorcan beusedfor thedeterminationof trypsin. As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity was gradually decreased with increasing trypsin concentration, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency was linearly related to the trypsin concentration in the range of 10–140 ng/mL (Fig. S10 B). The calibration curve can be expressed as y = 0.05353 + 0.00536x with correlation coefficient of 0.9908. The limit of detection for trypsin was calculated to be 4.3 ng/mL (3σ/slope). We compared this strategy with other methods for detecting protamine and trypsin (Tables S1 and S2). The detection limit values of this strategy were lower than that of the most reported methods and no complex surface modifications was required.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity for the detection of protamine and trypsin
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the specificity of the sensor for protamine detection, the effects of other biologically relevant substances such as metal ions, biomolecules and reducing agents were studied under the same experimental conditions as protamine determination, including Na+, K+, Ca2+, L-Arg, L-His, L-Cys, D-glucose, Dopamine and GSH. As shown in Fig. S11 A, under the same experimental conditions, protamine could significantly recover the fluorescence of N,S-CDs, while other interference substances exhibited only low effects. In addition, to verify the selectivity of this method for trypsin detection, some substances coexisting with trypsin in the biological systems were also examined, including glucose oxidase (GOx), urease, hyaluronidase, tyrosinase, elastase, pepsin, alkaline phosphatase (ALP), lysozyme and Horseradish peroxidase (HRP). Fig. S11 B showed that these substances hardly affected the detection of trypsin. Thus, the above results clearly show that the established method has excellent selectivity and reliability for the detection of protamine and trypsin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of protamine and trypsin in urine
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed method in the detection of actual samples, we used it to detect protamine and trypsin in diluted human urine samples (1%). The N,S-CDs/AuNPs sensing platform was applied to the detection of protamine and trypsin in three different human urine samples by standard addition methods. As shown in Table 2 and Table 3 , the recoveries of protamine and trypsin reached to 95.2%~107.6% and 95.6%~102.9%, respectively, indicating that the proposedsensorhasa greatpotentialto be used for the detection of protamine and trypsin in actual samples. In addition, we also used a commercial protamine ELISA kit to test the protamine in the human urine samples. The protamine tested by ELISA kit was consistent with the nanosensor (Table 2), which proved the accuracy of the nanosensor.

In summary, wesuccessfully established a novel “off–on-off” fluorescent nanosensor for protamine and trypsin detection based on IFE between N,S-CDs and AuNPs. AuNPs could easily quench the fluorescence of N,S-CDs as a result of IFE. After introducing of protamine, the fluorescence of N,S-CDs recovered due to the interaction between protamine and AuNPs. Trypsin specifically acts on peptide bonds containing arginine and lysine carboxyl groups, which can hydrolyze protamine, causing the deaggregation of AuNPs and further quenching the fluorescence of N,S-CDs. The concentrations of protamine and trypsin were measured by monitoring the fluorescence intensity changes of N,S-CDs. More importantly, the novel “off–on-off” fluorescent nanosensor has been successfully used to quantify protamine and trypsin in human urine samples, proving its potential application prospects in clinical diagnosis and evaluation.

Qiaozhen Bao: Methodology, Validation, Investigation. Dan Lin: Methodology, Validation, Investigation. Yaoran Gao: Methodology, Validation, Investigation. Lina Wu: . Jinhua Fu: . Khuslen Galaa: Methodology, Validation, Investigation. Xinhua Lin: . Liqing Lin: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21775023), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2019 J01308), Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province (2017Y9124).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106409.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
